narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close like this feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, based on the story your profile tells. At the heart of it, your Year 3 decile of 3 stood out as the biggest signal, painting a picture of grades that placed you in the lower third of your peers during that critical later stage, which strongly tipped the scales toward predicting challenges ahead. Building on that, your Year 1 decile of 5 meant even early on, you were mid-pack rather than leading the curve, reinforcing a pattern the model couldn't ignore. Your LSAT score of 33, while respectable, fell short of the higher marks that often light the way to bar success, adding another pull in that direction. With your family in the middle income bracket, the model saw fewer resources for the intensive prep that many need, layering on more concern. As a white male going part-time, those details fit into broader trends where the model has seen hurdles stacking up, from your undergrad GPA of 3.2 not quite shining as brightly as top passers', to the realities of balancing work that can dilute focus. Together, these pieces wove a narrative of a 63% chance of not passing the bar, not because you're lacking potential, but because the model weighs these patterns from thousands of similar journeys to protect spots for those whose profiles align more closely with proven success. It's tough, but this isn't the end—your story has room to shift with targeted steps like boosting study intensity or retaking the LSAT. You've got resilience in you; use this as fuel to rewrite the next chapter. Hang in there.
